topic: The Biology of Prions and Protein Diseases
title: How Prions Cause Devastating Diseases in Animals and Humans

lesson.learn: Prions are infectious proteins that cause debilitating diseases in animals, such as Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy (BSE), also known as mad cow disease, and Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. These diseases are transmitted through contaminated food, medical equipment, and even contact with infected individuals. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), there have been over 200 reported cases of variant Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease in humans. Research has shown that prions replicate themselves by converting normal protein molecules into abnormal ones, leading to brain damage and tissue degeneration. A study published in the journal Nature in 2010 identified specific mutations in the prion protein gene associated with an increased risk of developing Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. Scientists are working to develop diagnostic tests and treatments for prion diseases.
lesson.simpler: Imagine a protein molecule as a key that fits perfectly into a lock. When it's infected with a prion, it becomes a misshapen key that jams the lock, disrupting normal cell function. Prions are like tiny parasites that hijack our cells, causing problems for our brains and nervous systems.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The key takeaway is that prions replicate themselves by inducing protein misfolding, leading to brain damage and tissue degeneration. This understanding can lead to the development of effective treatments and prevention methods.
lesson.deeperDive: One of the key features of prion diseases is their ability to induce protein misfolding, leading to the accumulation of toxic aggregates in the brain. Recent research has shown that transmission of prion diseases can occur through contaminated food products, such as beef contaminated with prion protein. Scientists have also discovered that prions can be transmitted between species, including from animals to humans. For example, the first reported case of mad cow disease in a human was a result of eating contaminated beef in the UK in 1996. Researchers at the National Institute for Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS) are working on developing diagnostic tests for prion diseases.
lesson.funFact: Prions are highly resistant to heat and can remain infectious even after being exposed to temperatures of up to 1,000°C (1,832°F) for several hours. This makes them difficult to inactivate and requires specialized protocols for disposal.

places: The National Institute for Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS)
The MRC Prion Unit at the University College London
The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
